You have aSKed wnether Mayor Pnillis Moore may 
participate in city council decisions on: 
1. 
An application by Mr. and Mrs. Hills for a density 
designation change in the City of Napa General Plan. 
Mayor 
Moore's adult son has been hired to act as a consultant to Mr. 
and Mrs. Hills in connection with the project for whicn the 
density change is sought. 
2. 
A proposal to amena the General Plan density designation 
for a parcel of vacant property located next to a conaominium in 
whicn the Mayor's residence is located. 
Government Code Section 871001/ requires a public official 
to disqualify herself from voting on, or particlpating in, any 
decision in which she has a financial interest. 
Section 87103 
defines tne term financial interest: 
An official has a financial interest in a decision 
within the meaning of Section 87100 if it is reasonably 
foreseeable that the decision will have a material 
financlal effect, distinguisnable from its effect on 
the public generally, on: 
(a) 
Any business entity in which the public 
official has a direct or inalrect investment worth 
more than one thousand dollars ($1,000). 
1/ All statutory references made are to the 
Government Code unless otherwise noted. 


Elizabeth Strauss 
May 2 0, 1983 
page 2 
(b) 
Any real property in which the public 
official has a direct or 1ndirect investment worth 
more than one thousand dollars ($1,000). 
(c) 
Any source of income, other than loans 
by a commercial lending institution in the regular 
course of business on terms ava1lable to the 
public without regard to official status, aggre-
gat1ng two hundred fifty dollars ($250) or more in 
value provided to, received by or promised to the 
pUblic official within 12 months prior to the time 
when the decision is made. 
(d) 
Any business entity in which the public 
official 1S a director, officer, partner, trustee, 
employee, or holds any position of management. 
The facts you have presented in your first question do not, 
by themselves, indicate any potential conflict of interest under 
the Act. 
If the Mayor's only connection with the proposed 
density designation charge is the fact that her adult son is a 
consultant to Mr. and Mrs. Hills, sne does not nave to 
disqualify herself from the decision on the charge. 
The Act 
does not require disqualification of an official from a decision 
merely because the decision may nave a financial effect on the 
official's adult child. 
The second situation described in your advice request does 
present a potential conflict of 1nterest. 
From tne facts, I 
assume that Mayor Moore has an investment interest of over 
$1,000 in her condominium and in the common area in which the 
condominium development is located. 
This constitutes an 
interest in real property within the meaning of the Act. 
Section 82033. 
Therefore, if it is reasonably foreseeable that 
the proposed change in the density designation of the adjacent 
city-owned parcel will have a material financial effect on the 
value of her condominium, or on the value of the common area 
owned by condominium development, she must disqualify herself 
from that decision. 
From the facts you have provided, I am unable to determine 
whetner or not the proposed density change will have a material 
financial effect on the value of the Mayor's condominium, or on 
the value of the common area owned OJ the condominium 
development. 
I suggest that you consult Commission regulation 
18702 (b) (2)

In my capacity as City Attorney of the City of Napa, Mayor Phyllis Moore asked 
that I ask for an advice letter in response to the assertion that she should refrain from 
participating in two matters currently before the City Council because of a "conflict 
interest" • 
Each of these matters will be discussed by the City Council on May 31, 1983. 
Your advice prior to that date would facilitate the Mayor's action on this matter. 
Thank you very much for your attention. 
If you need additional facts or 
clarification about either of these matters, please give me a call. 
Best regards, 
Elizabeth

sections.question: 1. Whether Mayor Phyllis Moore may participate in city council decisions regarding an application by Mr. and Mrs. Hills for a density designation change, where Mayor Moore's adult son has been hired as a consultant to Mr. and Mrs. Hills in connection with the project. 2. Whether Mayor Moore may participate in a proposal to amend the General Plan density designation for a parcel of vacant property located next to a condominium in which the Mayor's residence is located.
sections.conclusion: On the first question, the Mayor does not have to disqualify herself from the decision on the density designation change, as the Act does not require disqualification merely because a decision may have a financial effect on an official's adult child. On the second question, the Mayor must disqualify herself if it is reasonably foreseeable that the proposed density change will have a material financial effect on the value of her condominium or the common area of the condominium development. The requester should consult Commission regulation 18702(b)(2) and potentially local real estate brokers or the City or County Assessor to determine whether a material financial effect is probable.
sections.question_synthetic: May Mayor Phyllis Moore participate in two city council decisions: (1) approving a density designation change for property where her adult son is acting as a consultant, and (2) amending the density designation of adjacent vacant land next to her condominium?
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The Mayor may participate in the first decision regarding her son's consultant work, as the Act does not require disqualification based on effects to adult children. However, she must disqualify herself from the second decision regarding the adjacent property if the density change would have a material financial effect on her condominium's value, which requires further factual analysis.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Document consists of incoming request letter (May 16, 1983) followed by FPPC response letter (May 20, 1983) from Barbara A. Milman, Chief of Legal Division. Both sections extracted; FPPC advice letter is primary document analyzed. Minor OCR errors in original document (e.g., 'particlpating' for 'participating') preserved in extraction.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: 1. Whether Mayor Phyllis Moore may participate in city council decisions regarding an application by Mr. and Mrs. Hills for a density designation change, where Mayor Moore's adult son has been hired as a consultant to Mr. and Mrs. Hills in connection with the project. 2. Whether Mayor Moore may participate in a proposal to amend the General Plan density designation for a parcel of vacant property located next to a condominium in which the Mayor's residence is located.

CONCLUSION: On the first question, the Mayor does not have to disqualify herself from the decision on the density designation change, as the Act does not require disqualification merely because a decision may have a financial effect on an official's adult child. On the second question, the Mayor must disqualify herself if it is reasonably foreseeable that the proposed density change will have a material financial effect on the value of her condominium or the common area of the condominium development. The requester should consult Commission regulation 18702(b)(2) and potentially local real estate brokers or the City or County Assessor to determine whether a material financial effect is probable.

embedding.summary: The FPPC advises that Mayor Phyllis Moore need not disqualify herself from a city council decision regarding a density designation change where her adult son serves as a consultant, but must disqualify herself from voting on amending the density designation of vacant property adjacent to her condominium if the change would materially affect her property's value.
